content.verdict: Maitai Polynesia is the answer to the question every Bora Bora honeymooner eventually asks: 'Is there any way to do this without spending $8,000?' Yes — and this is it. Straddling a thin strip of land between Matira Beach (the best public beach on Bora Bora, hands down) and the open lagoon, Maitai is a 72-room three-star property with a mix of beach rooms, garden rooms and — crucially — 30 overwater bungalows at roughly one-third the price of the chains. The overwater bungalows are older, simpler, smaller than Four Seasons or St. Regis, and they do not have butlers or glass floors or plunge pools. What they do have: the same lagoon underneath, the same Mount Otemanu view, the same ladder straight into impossibly blue water. For honeymooners who care more about where they are than how new the furniture is, this is a legitimately romantic stay. The beachfront restaurant, Haere Mai, is quite good; breakfast on the Matira side is a highlight. Service is friendly and unpretentious. This is not a luxury property — mini-fridge, no butler, basic bathroom, shared beaches — but it is the most honest value in Bora Bora and still delivers 80% of the emotional payoff of a stay at triple the price.
content.best_room: Splurge on the Overwater Bungalow (Premium) for the full Bora Bora honeymoon moment — ladder to the lagoon, private deck, Otemanu view. If budget is truly tight, the Beach Bungalow Matira is the best non-overwater option in all of Bora Bora: direct toes-in-sand access to Matira Beach, which is the public beach most magazines photograph. Skip the Standard Garden rooms for a honeymoon.
caveats: This is a three-star hotel, not a luxury resort — expect dated rooms, modest bathrooms and limited amenities (no spa, small pool or none depending on category).
The overwater bungalows are older and smaller than Hilton/Pearl — if that matters to you, budget up.
The property has two sides separated by the road (beach side and overwater side); you will walk across.
No butler service, no turndown, no minibar stocking.
It is excellent value, not a luxury experience.

faqs.q: Is Maitai Polynesia good for a honeymoon?
faqs.a: Yes, if your priority is Bora Bora at a reachable price. You get Matira Beach access and an authentic overwater bungalow experience. You do not get luxury service, a spa, or modern villa design. Couples who care about location more than amenities love it. Couples expecting Four Seasons service will be disappointed.

faqs.q: How much is an overwater bungalow here vs. the big resorts?
faqs.a: Typically $600-$900 per night at Maitai versus $1,500-$3,000 at Conrad, Four Seasons, St. Regis, Le Bora Bora. Over a 7-night stay that is a $6,000-$15,000 delta.

faqs.q: Can we walk to restaurants and shops?
faqs.a: Yes — this is one of the only Bora Bora hotels on the main island (not a private motu), so you can walk to Matira Point restaurants, Bloody Mary's via taxi, and small shops. That is genuinely rare on Bora Bora.

faqs.q: Is Matira Beach really that good?
faqs.a: Yes. It is consistently ranked among the best beaches in the South Pacific — white sand, shallow, swimmable, sunset-facing, and technically public. Staying at Maitai means stepping directly onto it.

faqs.q: Do overwater bungalows have glass floor panels?
faqs.a: Some do, some don't — ask at booking. The newer premium overwater bungalows have them; older ones do not.

faqs.q: What about transfers from the airport?
faqs.a: Included free shuttle boat from Motu Mute airport to Vaitape, then short bus to the hotel. This is a major cost saving versus resorts that charge $150-$250 per couple for transfers.
